title: How Google’s New Gemini Rates Work and How to Track Your Usage
gist: Google has overhauled how it meters Gemini AI usage—switching from a count of requests to the compute cost of each request across Free, Plus, Pro, and Ultra tiers—making limits opaque for users while letting Google manage data-center resource costs and reserve the right to throttle free users first.

insights.claim: Google now measures Gemini usage by the computing power each request requires rather than the number of requests, so a user might generate only two complex videos where they previously could make three simpler ones.
insights.speaker: David Nield
insights.so_what: Compute-based metering aligns pricing to Google's actual data-center cost, a template competitors may follow as AI margins tighten.
insights.locator: Google now measures Gemini AI usage

insights.claim: The new metering makes limits vague for end users who can no longer rely on rules like '5 image generations a day', with Google stating access may be limited based on testing, experimentation, or availability.
insights.speaker: David Nield
insights.so_what: Opaque, variable limits shift predictability risk onto users and signal Google prioritizing cost control over UX clarity.
insights.quote: access is subject to change or may be limited based on testing, experimentation or availability
insights.locator: What’s more, Google says in its support docs

insights.claim: Usage depends on three factors: the subscription plan, the complexity and length of prompts, and which Gemini model is selected, with faster more-complex models consuming more credits.
insights.speaker: David Nield
insights.so_what: Multi-variable pricing complexity foreshadows industry-wide move toward consumption-based AI billing.
insights.locator: The two main factors that go into how much AI

insights.claim: Google offers four paid Gemini tiers in the US—AI Plus at $8/month, AI Pro at $20/month, and AI Ultra at $100 or $200/month—plus a free tier, with usage scaling 2x on Plus, 4x on Pro, and 5x or 20x above Pro on Ultra relative to undefined 'standard' free limits.
insights.speaker: David Nield
insights.so_what: The steep 20x Ultra multiplier reveals how Google monetizes heavy AI users while keeping free-tier limits deliberately vague.
insights.locator: If you’re in the US, you’ve got four Gemini AI subscriptions

insights.claim: Context window size scales sharply by tier: 32K tokens (~24,000 words) for free users, 128K (~96,000 words) for Plus, and one million tokens (~750,000 words) for Pro and Ultra.
insights.speaker: David Nield
insights.so_what: Context window is a key upsell lever; the 30x gap between free and Pro shows where Google gates its most valuable capability.
insights.locator: The final difference between these models is context window size

insights.claim: Gemini enforces two limits—a current-usage bar resetting every five hours and a weekly limit—and paid users who hit them are demoted to the most basic AI model until the next reset.
insights.speaker: David Nield
insights.so_what: Even paying users face model downgrades at the cap, a friction point that could push power users toward competitors.

insights.claim: Google's support documents state limits may change without notice due to capacity constraints and that free users may be affected first if Google needs to manage AI resources.
insights.speaker: David Nield
insights.so_what: Prioritizing paid users during capacity crunches signals AI compute scarcity shaping product tiering decisions.
insights.locator: You will, inevitably, see offers to upgrade
